items.title: Linear fit to a quadratic relationship
items.scenario: True relationship: house price grows quadratically with square footage. You fit ordinary least-squares linear regression on 10,000 listings. Training R² is mediocre and validation error stays high no matter how many rows you add.
items.explanation: The line cannot bend; E[ŷ] systematically under- or over-predicts at the ends. That consistent structural miss is high bias — fixing it requires a nonlinear model (polynomial features, tree, neural net), not more rows alone.

items.title: Depth-1 decision stump on XOR-like pattern
items.scenario: Binary labels depend on whether two features have the same sign (XOR). A decision stump splits on one axis only; it cannot represent the checkerboard boundary.
items.explanation: Every training set yields a stump that misclassifies roughly half the pattern the same way. High bias from an hypothesis class that cannot express the true decision surface.

items.title: Tiny neural network on image digits
items.scenario: MNIST with a single hidden layer of 8 units. Train loss plateaus well above zero; train and test accuracy both stall around 85% while a deeper CNN reaches 99%.
items.explanation: Limited capacity caps what E[ŷ] can approximate — underfitting from high bias. Deeper/wider architecture reduces bias by expanding the function class.

items.title: Heavy L2 regularization on logistic regression
items.scenario: Spam classifier with C = 0.001 (very strong L2). Coefficients shrink toward zero; model predicts near-constant probability for most emails.
items.explanation: Regularization pulls the solution toward a simple, nearly constant predictor — increasing bias to gain stability. Loosening C reduces bias until variance becomes the limiting factor.

items.title: Missing key feature in a forecasting model
items.scenario: Predict daily ice-cream sales using temperature only, omitting "is_weekend". The model consistently under-predicts weekend spikes every week.
items.explanation: Omitted structure creates systematic error in E[ŷ] at weekends — bias from mis-specification (wrong features), not random noise across resampled training sets.
